comments_critique.summary: The discussion centers around a physical implementation of Conway's Game of Life, with commenters sharing admiration for the project, suggestions for cheaper alternatives, and reflections on the nature of physical versus digital realizations. Some express fascination with related technologies and propose enhancements or alternative approaches.

comments.author: kangalioo
comments.text: A much cheaper way might have been to buy a couple of Novation Launchpads. 8x8 full RGB tactile buttons for 90€, MIDI-controllable. Four of those next to another for 16x16 at 360€ plus a little bit for cables and controller comes out at 1/3 the price
comments.analysis: Provides a practical and cost-effective alternative with specific product suggestions, demonstrating knowledge of available hardware.

comments.author: kanapala
comments.text: Technology Will Save Us, from a brighter past, produced the Arcade Coder as their swan song. It had 12×12 tactile buttons with color leds inside, a companion app to program it with scratch like code blocks, and interactive tutorials. It's ESP32 based. There's an effort to continue it's life, ast the app was on the app store for a brief time. https://github.com/padraigfl/twsu-arcade-coder-esp32
comments.analysis: Shares relevant historical context and a similar project, adding depth and potential resources for interested builders.

comments.author: smcameron
comments.text: Something like those switches might be made very cheaply with a 3D printer, possibly a laser cutter, some transparent or semitransparent acrylic sheet, tactile switches and some LEDs. I designed a cheapo replacement for $50 tellite switches and got the price down to about $0.60 Not quite the same, as these are a lot bigger, and getting things down to the desired size might be troublesome. Anyway, here's a little video of my fake cheapo tellite switches: https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=LaenrgPVCjc
comments.analysis: Offers a feasible DIY approach to reduce costs using accessible fabrication methods, showing technical insight.

comments.author: MrGilbert
comments.text: I wonder if going for keyboard switches with RGB could bring the price down, if you then either print the keycaps yourself, our use a 3d printing service. 23 Cherry MX switches cost 20€, that‘s roughly 260€ for a 17x17 matrix.
comments.analysis: Suggests an alternative component choice to lower expenses, reflecting practical knowledge of hardware pricing.

comments.author: alnwlsn
comments.text:
Wow, in switches alone this would cost me $1200! Which actually isn't that bad for a one off-like this with easily available components - I've seen worse. You can of course take your chances with Aliexpress Specials but be warned: some of those cheap switches are cheap switches (sometimes, they're barely even switches). And you won't be getting them inside of 2 weeks.

I think I would have tried mechanical keyboarding switches that can take an LED and a clear keycap. Not really the same effect, but you could probably have gotten out to 24x24 for the same price.
comments.analysis: Provides a realistic cost estimate and shares caution about cheap components, adding valuable perspective on component quality.

comments.author: eps
comments.text: I saw one in a computer museum in Switzerland. It was a much larger field, it was just large orange LEDs (or were they tubes?), but it also cycled between a dozen of different cell automata games. Something about being able to see individual "pixels" made it really mesmerizing.
comments.analysis: Relates personal experience with similar installations, enriching the discussion with real-world examples.

comments.author: slow_typist
comments.text:
Très cool.

A grid of capacitive touch sensors could be printed directly on the pcb, bringing down costs by a degree of magnitude. Real switches are much more satisfying though.
comments.analysis: Suggests a technical improvement to reduce cost while acknowledging the tactile appeal of physical switches.

comments.author: self_awareness
comments.text: That's not a "physical" version of game of life -- that's a digital version, like every version, but with bigger pixels.
comments.analysis: Offers a conceptual critique distinguishing digital from truly physical implementations, adding depth to the discussion.

comments.author: alexb_
comments.text: This isn't what this is about, but I wonder if anyone has created a fully mechanized version of Conway's Game of Life. The fact that you only have to follow 4 rules, and each cell only needs to observe its neighbors to follow those rules, makes me wonder if it's possible to track these things mechanically. I unfortunately don't know enough about mechanical engineering to do such a thing.
comments.analysis: Poses an interesting engineering question about mechanical realizations, showing curiosity and understanding of the game's rules.

comments.author: bawolff
comments.text:
> To bring the idea to life, I started with rigorous budgeting: I figured out what would be a reasonable amount to spend on the project and then multiplied that by 10.

Lol, this is the way to do hobbies
comments.analysis: Humorous and relatable comment reflecting on the budgeting approach, adding lightness to the conversation.
